sanskrit_devanagari:
अच्छेद्योऽयमदाह्योऽयमक्लेद्योऽशोष्य एव च ।
नित्यः सर्वगतः स्थाणुरचलोऽयं सनातनः ॥ २४ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
acchedyo 'yam adāhyo 'yam akledyo 'śoṣya eva ca |
nityaḥ sarva-gataḥ sthāṇur acalo 'yaṁ sanātanaḥ || 24 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (transliteration body identical; Mukundananda house-style aspirate marks).
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs).
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.024 = MBh_06,024.024). Body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: stable / fixed (like a stump or pillar)

translations.verbatim_quote: This individual soul is unbreakable and insoluble, and can be neither burned nor dried. He is everlasting, present everywhere, unchangeable, immovable and eternally the same.
The soul is unbreakable and incombustible; it can neither be dampened nor dried. It is everlasting, in all places, unalterable, immutable, and primordial.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: All these qualifications of the atomic soul definitely prove that the individual soul is eternally the atomic particle of the spirit whole, and he remains the same atom eternally, without change.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Since this is so, therefore ayam, It; acchedyah, cannot be cut. Since the other elements which are the causes of destruction of one another are not capable of destroying this Self, therefore It is nityah, eternal. Being eternal, It is sarva-gatah, omnipresent. Being omnipresent, It is sthanuh, stationary, fixed like a stump.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 2.24 as the affirmative restatement of 2.23 plus four additional properties. The first four (acchedya, adāhya, akledya, aśoṣya) are the four denials of 2.23 stated as positive attributes. The next four (nitya, sarva-gata, sthāṇu, acala) form a deductive chain: because the elements cannot destroy It, It is eternal; because eternal, all-pervading; because all-pervading, stationary (no place to move to); because stationary, immovable. Sanātana ('primordial') closes the list as a temporal property — the Self is what has always been.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Weapons, fire, water and air are incapable of cleaving, burning, wetting and drying the self; for, the nature of the self is to pervade all elements; It is present everywhere; for, It is subtler than all the elements; It is not capable of being pervaded by them.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja groups 2.23 and 2.24 as a single argument keyed off the Self's pervasive subtlety. The four invulnerabilities follow because the Self pervades all elements (sarva-gata) and is subtler than them. Eternity, all-pervading-ness, stability, immovability, and primordiality are not separate properties to be derived independently; they are different angles on the single property of being subtler than every medium that could otherwise act on the Self.

positions.rendering: unchangeable, immovable and eternally the same

deductive chain: because eternal, all-pervading; because all-pervading, stationary (sthāṇu — fixed like a stump, no place to move to); because stationary, immovable (acala); sanātana closes as temporal-primordial
the eight properties are angles on a single property: the Self is subtler than every medium

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Modern translations preserve the eight-property list but flatten the structural argument. Shankara reads the list as a deductive chain (eternal → all-pervading → stationary → immovable) terminating in sanātana as the temporal capstone. Ramanuja reads the list as eight angles on a single fact (subtlety/pervasion). Both readings agree that 'sthāṇu' (stake/stump) is a structural-stability metaphor, distinct from 'acala' (no spatial movement) — they are not synonyms. The English translations collapse 'sthāṇu' and 'acala' into a single 'unchangeable/immovable' pair, losing the distinction between 'fixed because there is nowhere else to be' (sthāṇu) and 'not subject to motion' (acala).

literal_meaning: This (Self) is uncleavable, incombustible, unwettable, and undryable. It is eternal, all-pervading, stable, immovable, and primordial.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The technical specification of the imperishable. Following the four denials of 2.23, Krishna restates them affirmatively (acchedya, adāhya, akledya, aśoṣya) and adds five further properties: nitya (eternal), sarva-gata (all-pervading), sthāṇu (stable), acala (immovable), sanātana (primordial). The four denials and the five additions together name nine properties. Shankara reads them as a deductive chain plus a temporal capstone; Ramanuja reads them as angles on subtlety/pervasion. Either way, the verse functions as the technical spec sheet for what the Self is — a list precise enough that any candidate substance can be checked against the list and confirmed or rejected as the imperishable.
